parsed.summary: The cumulative v0.4.0→v0.4.6 diff introduces 84 files with +6623/−480 lines, including a full retry/backoff subsystem and BigModel error dispatch. Three material concerns block confidence: (1) a deleted module (process.mjs) whose consumers may not be fully migrated, risking runtime ReferenceError on uncommon code paths; (2) no UAT evidence for v0.4.6 despite it shipping the highest-risk change (automatic retry on API calls) that specifically needs failure-path validation; (3) a 203-line retry module paired with a 168-line error classifier that together implement automatic retries with no visible idempotency guard, risking duplicate job submissions under transient failure. The breadth of changes and the iterative multi-round hotfix history (Bundles C, D3+, F) suggest the codebase was still stabilizing during this window.

findings.title: Deleted process.mjs module may leave dangling imports on untested code paths
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs (67 lines) was fully deleted in the diff stat. Commit 225401a describes it as part of a 'full thread/resume scaffolding purge' fixing a ReferenceError. While scripts/check-imports.mjs was modified (5 lines changed) to account for the removal, import-checking scripts typically verify static imports at the top level. They do not catch dynamic imports, eval-based loading, or references from markdown command files that may invoke purged functionality at runtime. Any remaining reference to the deleted module will throw ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND only when that specific code path executes — a classic latent defect that passes CI but fails in production under specific user actions.
findings.recommendation: Run `git grep -r 'process\.mjs\|lib/process' -- '*.mjs' '*.js' '*.md' '*.json'` against the current HEAD to confirm zero remaining references. If any are found in command markdown or dynamically-loaded paths, add a defensive guard. Consider adding a dedicated CI check that greps for imports of deleted modules.

findings.title: No UAT evidence for v0.4.6 — retry/backoff and error dispatch are unvalidated under failure conditions
findings.body: The test-automation/uat-reports/ directory contains v0.4.4/ and v0.4.5/ subdirectories with structured scenario JSON files and run scripts, but no v0.4.6/ directory exists. The v0.4.6 release (commit e0a9ba8) added 'BigModel error dispatch + auto retry/backoff' — features that by definition alter behavior under API failure, timeout, and rate-limit conditions. These are the exact scenarios where silent misbehavior is most dangerous and most likely: retrying a non-idempotent operation, misclassifying a fatal error as retryable, or exhausting retries without surfacing the original error. The absence of UAT means these failure paths have no end-to-end validation evidence.
findings.recommendation: Before shipping, create test-automation/uat-reports/v0.4.6/ with scenarios that exercise: (a) a transient 429/503 from BigModel to verify retry fires correctly, (b) a persistent 5xx to verify retry exhaustion surfaces a clear error rather than silently swallowing it, (c) a malformed API response to verify the error classifier doesn't throw uncaught, (d) a concurrent job submission during retry to verify no duplicate creation.

findings.title: New retry.mjs + bigmodel-errors.mjs implement automatic retries with no visible idempotency key or deduplication mechanism
findings.body: scripts/lib/retry.mjs (203 new lines) and scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 new lines) together classify BigModel API errors and automatically retry retryable ones. scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs (155 lines changed) consumes both. The risk: if the BigModel API calls that trigger retries are non-idempotent (e.g., job submission, review creation), a transient network timeout followed by a successful retry could result in duplicate operations. The test file tests/retry.test.mjs (352 lines) is substantial but the diff stat alone cannot confirm it covers duplicate-operation scenarios. Commit e0a9ba8's description ('auto retry/backoff') suggests the retry is transparent to the caller, meaning the caller has no opportunity to guard against duplication.
findings.recommendation: Audit every call site in glm-client.mjs that uses the retry wrapper. For any call that performs a write/submission (not a read), either: (a) add an idempotency key to the request, (b) wrap the call in a deduplication guard that checks for existing results before allowing the retry, or (c) make retry opt-in for write operations with explicit documentation of the risk. Add a test that sends a write operation through the retry layer with a simulated transient failure and asserts exactly one side-effect occurred.

findings.title: State and job-control modules modified concurrently create window for race-condition regression
findings.body: Four stateful modules were modified in the same release window: scripts/lib/state.mjs (32 lines changed), scripts/lib/job-control.mjs (20 lines changed), scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed), and scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs (155 lines changed). If these modules coordinate job lifecycle state (creation → running → completion/failure) through shared filesystem state — which the 'tracked-jobs' naming and job-control naming strongly imply — then concurrent invocations could race on state transitions. The commit history shows iterative bug-fix rounds (Bundles C, D3+, F) which is consistent with state-machine bugs that are hard to catch in sequential tests. No lock file, flock, or atomic rename pattern is visible from the diff stat to suggest concurrency protection.
findings.recommendation: Inspect state.mjs, job-control.mjs, and tracked-jobs.mjs for concurrency guards (atomic file writes via rename, lock files, or optimistic concurrency via version stamps). If none exist, add explicit flock-based locking around state mutations or use atomic rename (write to temp file, then rename). Add a test that runs two concurrent operations and asserts both complete without state corruption.

next_steps: Run `git grep -r 'process\.mjs\|lib/process'` on HEAD to confirm the deleted module has zero remaining references across all file types
Create v0.4.6 UAT scenarios covering retry on transient failure, retry exhaustion, error misclassification, and concurrent job submission
Audit every glm-client.mjs call site that uses the retry wrapper to classify it as read vs. write; for write calls, add idempotency guards
Inspect state.mjs, job-control.mjs, and tracked-jobs.mjs for filesystem-level concurrency protection; if absent, add atomic writes or locking before shipping
